text: The surgical team uses a 10 mm working port on the right side. The left lobe of the liver is visible, showing puckered metastatic lesions. Diagnostic laparoscopy reveals no other grossly visible metastases.
The surgeon identifies the diaphragm and caudate lobe, and begins separating peritoneal adhesions near the inferior vena cava (IVC).

The surgeon frees lateral adhesions of the left lobe of the liver, focusing on the flimsy left triangular ligament. The caudate lobe and inferior vena cava (IVC) are visible, along with the stomach showing a mild hiatus.

The surgeon dissects the right triangular ligament and central part of the falciform ligament during a left lateral hepatectomy. The inferior vena cava (IVC) is visible, along with a portion of the suprahepatic cava.

The surgeon identifies the inferior vena cava (IVC) and the phrenic vein, noting the potential for significant bleeding if the phrenic vein is injured, and takes care to avoid it during the procedure.

The surgeon is dissecting the left hepatic vein, with the inferior vena cava (IVC) visible on the left side of the instrument. The left hepatic vein is identified as the site for the final transection, which will be performed intraparenchymally due to the tumor not encroaching on the vein.
The surgeon positions the stapler at the designated line of excision on the liver parenchyma, preparing to fire the staple. An assistant holds the tissue upward to facilitate the stapling process.
The surgeon identifies the segment three pedicle, highlighting its vascular structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies an artery and the portal vein of segment three. An instrument is prepared for use, and a Hem-o-lok clip is requested for vessel division.
The surgeon identifies the segment two and segment three pedicles, along with the artery, in the surgical field. These structures are visually distinct and positioned for resection.

The surgeon clips the left lateral lobe hepatic artery, followed by the segment three pedicle and segment two pedicle. The hepatic vein is prepared for stapling, and the liver parenchyma is addressed with a harmonic scalpel. The liver appears neither fatty nor fibrotic.
The surgeon prepares to use an Endo GIA cartridge to tackle the visible pedicle, demonstrating a method for vascular control during the hepatectomy procedure.

The surgeon performs parenchymal dissection using harmonic and clips, targeting the separate pedicles of segments two and three. If difficulty arises, staplers may be used to assist in the dissection.

The surgeon continues the liver resection by dividing the parenchyma using a harmonic scalpel. The harmonic device is employed in conjunction with the finger fracture method to separate liver tissue without initially applying energy. The surgeon emphasizes not dissecting too deeply to maintain control over hemostasis.
The surgeon uses the harmonic device to delineate a vessel during the hepatectomy, employing a finger-fracture technique to confirm the vessel's location before proceeding with dissection.
The surgeon requests a Hem-o-lok clip and Maryland dissector, preparing to view and manipulate the surgical site from below.

The surgeon controls a section of tissue that is oozing blood by reaching into the depth of the surgical field. To access this area, the surgeon divides the tissue, bringing it into the visual field for better management.

The surgeon uses a Powered Echelon Flex Endovascular White Stapler with a special curved beak cartridge to control inflow vessels. The stapler's beak accommodates the tissue securely, preventing vessel slippage during division.

The surgeon is dissecting the liver parenchyma near the ligamentum venosum, exposing a tributary of the left hepatic vein. The main proper left hepatic vein is visible, and preparations are being made to use a stapler for vessel control.

The surgeon completes the parenchymal transection during a left lateral hepatectomy, using a 35 vascular load to secure the left hepatic vein. The vessel is encircled to prevent accidental slippage, ensuring complete transaction. The phrenic vein and other structures are spared, and the main portal pedicle is not encircled during this dissection.

The surgeon completes the hepatectomy using a Powered Echelon Flex-XXXXX Endovascular White Stapler, ensuring no blood loss during the resection. The specimen is successfully removed, and the surgical field is clear. The team prepares for colonic mobilization.
